era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant political, technological, and cultural transformation across multiple civilisations. In Europe, the Renaissance is flourishing in Italy, and centralised nation-states are consolidating power. The Ming Dynasty in China is at its cultural and administrative peak. The Ottoman Empire is expanding into the Balkans. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire is expanding under Montezuma I. This era is marked by technological innovation, increased maritime exploration, and the beginning of European global expansion.

events.title: Council of Florence; union of Eastern and Western Christian churches (temporary)
events.description: The Council of Florence (1438–1445) was a major ecumenical council of the Catholic Church that attempted to reunite the Eastern Orthodox Church and the Roman Catholic Church, which had been in schism since 1054. In 1439, the council reached a temporary agreement on the union of the churches, known as the Decree of Union. The agreement was signed by Pope Eugenius IV and representatives of the Eastern Orthodox Church, including the Patriarch of Constantinople. However, the union was short-lived; it was rejected by many Orthodox Christians and formally repudiated after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453. The council is significant as an attempt at Christian reconciliation and as a reflection of the broader intellectual and diplomatic currents of the Renaissance.
key_figures: Pope Eugenius IV (Roman Catholic Pope, r. 1431–1447)
Patriarch Joseph II of Constantinople (Eastern Orthodox Patriarch)
Emperor John VIII Palaiologus (Byzantine Emperor, r. 1425–1448)

sources.name: Council of Florence official records and decrees

events.certainty_note: The Council of Florence and the Decree of Union in 1439 are well-documented in papal, conciliar, and Orthodox church records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Led to 1453-001 (Ottoman conquest of Constantinople; union repudiated)

events.title: Printing press development; Gutenberg's continued experimentation in Mainz
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg was actively developing his movable-type printing press in Mainz during 1439. This year falls within the period of intensive experimentation and refinement of the technology. Gutenberg's innovation combined the screw press (used in wine-making), movable metal type (known in East Asia for centuries), and oil-based inks into a practical system for mass-producing books in Europe. The exact date of the press's invention is debated among scholars, with estimates ranging from 1440 to 1450. The first documented printed books (the Gutenberg Bible) date to c. 1455.

sources.name: Indirect evidence from Gutenberg's later legal disputes and business records (1450s–1460s)
Historiographic consensus based on technical analysis

events.certainty_note: The exact year of the printing press's invention is uncertain. 1439 is within the estimated period of development, but scholars debate whether the invention occurred in 1440, 1441, or 1450. No contemporary documentation of the press's invention from 1439 survives.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration; continued expansion along the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued their systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1439. Portuguese ships were gradually pushing further south along the coast, establishing trading posts and gathering information about African geography, peoples, and resources. This period saw the beginning of Portuguese commercial interest in African gold, ivory, and other goods. The exploration was motivated by a combination of factors: the desire for wealth, the spread of Christianity, and the accumulation of geographical knowledge. These voyages laid the groundwork for the later Portuguese slave trade and European colonialism in Africa.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and administrative documents
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica dos Feitos da Guiné (Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea), written c. 1453–1460

events.certainty_note: Portuguese maritime exploration along the African coast was ongoing throughout the 1430s–1440s. Specific events in 1439 are less well-documented than the major raids and discoveries of 1441 and later years.
cross_references: Led to 1441-001 (Portuguese slave raid at Cape Bojador)

events.title: Ming Dynasty; Emperor Yingzong's reign begins; cultural and administrative consolidation
events.description: Emperor Yingzong ascended to the Ming throne in 1435 and continued his reign in 1439. His reign (r. 1435–1449, 1457–1464) was marked by cultural and administrative consolidation. The Ming Dynasty was at its height during this period, with a strong centralised government, a flourishing economy, and significant cultural achievements in art, literature, and architecture. However, Yingzong's reign also saw the gradual withdrawal of support for maritime exploration, as Confucian officials increasingly opposed the treasure voyages as wasteful. The Ming court was focused on internal consolidation and defence against Mongol incursions from the north.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records

events.certainty_note: Emperor Yingzong's reign and the general characteristics of the Ming Dynasty during this period are well-documented in Ming court records.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming Dynasty history (1368–1644)

events.title: Ottoman Empire; Murad II's military campaigns in the Balkans
events.description: Under Sultan Murad II (r. 1421–1444, 1446–1451), the Ottoman Empire continued its systematic conquest and consolidation of the Balkans in 1439. Ottoman forces were engaged in campaigns against Hungarian and Venetian strongholds in the region. The Ottomans were gradually eliminating Christian resistance and establishing Ottoman administrative control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. This period saw the transformation of the Ottoman state from a regional Anatolian power into a major Balkan and Mediterranean power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans during the 1430s–1440s are well-documented, though specific events in 1439 are less detailed than those of 1440–1441.
cross_references: Part of broader Ottoman expansion in the 15th century

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Florence's Decree of Union (1439-001) is sometimes presented as a major success in Christian reconciliation. However, the union was rejected by many Orthodox Christians and was short-lived, formally repudiated after 1453. The council's significance lies more in its attempt at reconciliation than in any lasting achievement. The dating of Gutenberg's press (1439-002) is highly uncertain; scholars debate whether the invention occurred in 1439, 1440, 1441, or 1450. Portuguese maritime exploration (1439-003) was ongoing but less dramatic than the major raids and discoveries of 1441 and later years.
historiographic_note: The year 1439 is well-documented for European, Ottoman, and East Asian events, reflecting the bias of surviving sources toward literate, centralised states. The Council of Florence is well-documented in papal and conciliar records, but the perspectives of ordinary Orthodox Christians and non-European peoples are largely absent. Portuguese exploration is documented in royal records and later chronicles, but African perspectives on Portuguese activities are not recorded in surviving sources. The historical record for 1439 is shaped by the priorities and biases of European, Ottoman, and Chinese chroniclers.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Florence's union was repudiated after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
Gutenberg's press development culminated in the printing of the Gutenberg Bible c. 1455.
Portuguese maritime exploration led to the slave raid at Cape Bojador in 1441.
Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans continued and intensified in 1440–1441.
